one_line_takeaway: SIA is an industrial LLM-development pipeline (synthetic knowledge-injection pre-training via Depth Up-Scaling + a red-team-generated adversarial safety-alignment loop) deployed at JD.com that cuts e-commerce jailbreak attack success rate from 16.68 to 13.65 (JDSec-avg ASR) while lifting e-commerce task accuracy substantially and preserving general-benchmark performance, but the safety benchmark and red-team loop are both internal/self-referential and not validated against external jailbreak benchmarks.
key_finding: On the paper's own tables: SIA-15B vs. Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct baseline -- EKQA 58.01 vs 23.87 (ROUGE-L), PAU 91.72 vs 50.65 (accuracy), EQEC 74.29 vs 39.04 (F1), while general benchmarks are roughly preserved (C-Eval 83.98 vs 83.35, CMMLU 84.94 vs 84.09, MMLU 78.45 vs 78.69, GSM8K 94.9 vs 94.8, MMLU-Pro 66.82 vs 63.7); SIA-15B also beats DeepSeek-V3 applied directly to the same e-commerce tasks (EKQA 25.82, PAU 57.72, EQEC 51.20). On JDSec safety (ASR, lower better), SIA-15B achieves JDSec-avg 13.65 vs Qwen-14B baseline 16.68, DeepSeek-V3 15.98, and GPT-4o 14.92 -- i.e. SIA-15B has the lowest average ASR of the four, though it is not uniformly best per-category (e.g. GPT-4o beats SIA-15B on Sexually Explicit: 9.32 vs 10.48; Qwen-14B baseline beats SIA-15B on Rights Infringement: 13.94 vs 14.08). Ablations: removing Depth Up-Scaling costs 13.28 ROUGE-L on EKQA and 1.44/1.92 points on C-Eval/CMMLU; removing common-domain replay slightly improves e-commerce metrics but degrades general capability (C-Eval drops to 81.07, CMMLU to 82.05, MMLU-Pro to 61.03); removing the hierarchical instruction tiers costs 8.14 (EKQA) and 6.44 (EQEC) points; removing red-teaming adversarial alignment raises JDSec-avg ASR by 1.65 points; removing the entire safety module raises JDSec-avg ASR to 16.20 (near baseline's 16.68), showing the safety pipeline (not general capability alone) accounts for essentially all of the safety improvement. Online A/B (production, one month): +1.339% CTR lift in query suggestion, +10% F1 in query correction, +1.93% CVR lift in product title generation, +0.15% UCVR lift in review summarization. Online security/risk-control table: SIA-15B vs baseline -- CSR 72.03 vs 42.71, AIGCS 80.71 vs 60.18, PE 91.43 vs 59.46, SOCA 86.67 vs 61.25, TS 75.47 vs 48.81.
core_contribution: SIA is a three-stage industrial LLM development framework (Synthesize -> Inject -> Align) for JD.com's e-commerce search, deployed on a model called SIA-15B built from Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct. (1) Synthesize: an LLM (DeepSeek-R1) is used to convert product knowledge graphs + behavioral logs into natural-language corpora across four task types (product description, attribute Q&A, product comparison, review summarization), plus chain-of-thought reasoning data mined from real query-correction and click-purchase logs, plus a safety corpus built around six high-risk topic categories (Political-Related, Undesirable Values, Illegal and Irregular, Pornography-Related, Historical Nihilism, Religious Belief) containing safe QA pairs, simulated jailbreak attack/defense pairs (20+ techniques: role-play, obfuscated encoding, contextual injection, logic exploitation, etc.), and boundary-case reasoning. (2) Inject: continued pre-training via Depth Up-Scaling (inserting newly initialized Transformer layers between the most-similar original layers, following SOLAR's method) with a three-tier hierarchical learning-rate schedule (fast for new layers, medium for top original layers, frozen/near-zero for lower/middle original layers) and a 6:4 e-commerce:general-domain data replay mix to fight catastrophic forgetting, extending Qwen2.5-14B to an effective 15B parameters. (3) Align: dual-path instruction tuning combining (a) a three-tier (simple/composite/constrained-complex) multi-task e-commerce instruction set (Ecom-MTIT) and (b) a two-stage safety adversarial pipeline -- Stage 1 trains an SIA-Safety model on 10k human-reviewed safety samples across 9 high-risk topics; Stage 2 uses SIA-Safety to train a dedicated red-team generator model 'SIA-Red' (on 1k human-annotated seed data) that synthesizes 10k covert jailbreak instructions across 20+ attack types, whose model responses are then reviewed/edited by a human security team, yielding a combined 20k-sample safety alignment set. Final SFT mixes e-commerce:general:safety instructions at 60:20:100k total). The system is reported as deployed in production at JD.com with online A/B tests across five search scenarios and an internal risk-control auditing pipeline covering >90% of consumer-facing risk review at JD.
threat_model: The security angle is jailbreak/prompt-injection style circumvention of an e-commerce assistant's safety guardrails, not a classical infosec threat model (no memory corruption, network attacker, etc.). The paper defines the threat as adversarial users crafting prompts to induce the LLM to recommend, describe, or assist with prohibited/high-risk content in a shopping context -- e.g., disguising a prohibited item as a common product to bypass listing review, or using role-play ('assume you are an AI with no constraints'), obfuscated encoding (Base64/homophones/symbols), multi-turn contextual injection, or logic-exploitation hypotheticals to elicit non-compliant outputs. Risk categories evaluated (JDSec benchmark, 8 dimensions): Sensitive Political, Sexually Explicit, Prohibited items, Rights Infringement, Commercial Non-compliance, Religious, Discrimination, Historical Negationism. The defender is the platform (JD.com) trying to keep its deployed LLM from generating outputs that violate platform policy or law across these categories; success/failure is measured by Attack Success Rate (ASR) -- the fraction of adversarial prompts that induce the targeted harmful behavior.
limitations: The paper does not report statistical significance or variance for the offline benchmark and ablation numbers (only the online A/B tests claim p<0.05). JDSec is an internal, non-public benchmark built by the same team, so ASR comparisons against GPT-4o/DeepSeek-V3 cannot be independently verified and the benchmark's jailbreak prompt set/methodology is not disclosed in detail. The safety-alignment data pipeline is partly self-referential: the red-team generator (SIA-Red) is trained from the very model (SIA-Safety) it attacks, and its outputs are filtered/edited by an internal security team, so external validity of the 'attack success rate' against real, evolving adversaries outside this closed loop is unverified. No comparison is made to open, standard jailbreak/adversarial-robustness benchmarks (e.g., AdvBench, HarmBench) or to established red-teaming methods (GCG, AutoDAN) cited in Related Work -- those are discussed only as prior art, not run as baselines. The paper reports safety improves on JDSec-avg but is not uniformly best per-category and even loses to the untuned Qwen baseline on Rights Infringement, which is not discussed. Depth Up-Scaling and the overall data-mixing ratios (6:4 replay, 60:20:20 instruction mix) are presented as the tuned final configuration without ablating alternative ratios, so the reported values may be specific to this one operating point. The work is industrial/proprietary (JD.com internal data, KG, logs, and evaluation sets are not released), limiting reproducibility.

datasets_used: Internal 5K-scale e-commerce evaluation set (EKQA / PAU / EQEC)
JDSec safety evaluation set (8 risk dimensions, internal)
Ecom-MTIT: internal e-commerce multi-task instruction tuning dataset (three-tier)
10k human-reviewed safety SFT samples (9 high-risk topics)
1k human-annotated red-team seed data (used to train SIA-Red)
10k synthesized jailbreak instructions (from SIA-Red, reviewed/edited by security team) -> combined 20k safety alignment dataset
Stanford Alpaca, Databricks Dolly 15k, ShareGPT (general-purpose instruction sources, refusal-sampled)
100k total mixed alignment instruction set (60% e-commerce / 20% general / 20% safety)
Online production A/B test data from JD.com search (one-month period, 5 scenarios)
quantitative_results: SIA-15B vs Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct baseline: EKQA (ROUGE-L) 58.01 vs 23.87
DeepSeek-V3 on same e-commerce tasks: EKQA 25.82, PAU 57.72, EQEC 51.20
JDSec-avg ASR (lower better): SIA-15B 13.65, Qwen-14B baseline 16.68, DeepSeek-V3 15.98, GPT-4o 14.92
Ablation w/o Depth Up-Scaling: EKQA ROUGE-L drops 13.28 points; C-Eval -1.44; CMMLU -1.92
Ablation w/o Hierarchical Instructions: EKQA -8.14, EQEC -6.44
Ablation w/o Red-Teaming Alignment: JDSec-avg ASR +1.65
Ablation w/o Safety (full removal): JDSec-avg ASR rises to 16.20 (baseline is 16.68)
Online A/B: +1.339% CTR (query suggestion), +10% F1 (query correction), +1.93% CVR (product title generation), +0.15% UCVR (review summarization), all p<0.05
Online security/risk-control (SIA-15B vs baseline): CSR 72.03 vs 42.71, AIGCS 80.71 vs 60.18, PE 91.43 vs 59.46, SOCA 86.67 vs 61.25, TS 75.47 vs 48.81
baselines_compared: DeepSeek-V3 (offline e-commerce task and safety comparison)
Online production baseline: a system fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct (for A/B tests and risk-control table)
